title: Inferior epigastric artery (rectus transection)
vignette: A 27-year-old woman, gravida 1 para 0, with gestational diabetes mellitus comes to the hospital at 38 weeks gestation for evaluation of abdominal pain. The patient is scheduled for an elective cesarean section at 39 weeks as a recent ultrasound revealed that the estimated fetal weight is 5200 g (11 lb 4 oz). Examination reveals that the patient is in active labor and the fetus is in breech presentation; an urgent cesarean section is performed. During the surgery, the rectus abdominus muscle is split vertically, but space appears to be limited. In anticipation of a large-for-gestational-age baby, a decision is made to transect the rectus abdominis muscle. If the rectus abdominis muscle is incised laterally, which of the following structures is at greatest risk of injury?

clues.note: Gestational diabetes predisposes to fetal macrosomia (a large-for-gestational-age baby), which is why extra surgical exposure — and a muscle transection — becomes necessary.
5200 g is well above the macrosomia threshold (~4000-4500 g). The big baby is the reason the surgeon must transect the rectus to gain room.

clues.text: rectus abdominus muscle is split vertically
clues.note: A midline vertical split between the two rectus bellies is the standard cesarean approach. Splitting vertically is bloodless; the danger arises only when the muscle is cut across (transected) laterally.

clues.text: the rectus abdominis muscle is incised laterally
clues.note: This is the decisive spatial detail. A LATERAL/transverse cut of the rectus threatens the vessel that enters the muscle at its lateral edge near the arcuate line — the inferior epigastric artery.

whatMatters: The clinical wrapper (macrosomia, breech, GDM) just explains WHY the rectus is transected. The testable point is pure anatomy: which structure runs where the rectus is cut LATERALLY? The inferior epigastric artery ascends on the posterior rectus and enters at its lateral aspect near the arcuate line — so a lateral transection puts it at greatest risk. Answer D.
strategy: For anterior abdominal wall questions, map the rectus sheath vessels: the SUPERIOR epigastric (from the internal thoracic) feeds the upper rectus; the INFERIOR epigastric (from the external iliac) climbs the posterior rectus and enters laterally near the arcuate line. Below the arcuate line there's no posterior sheath, so inferior epigastric injury bleeds freely (rectus sheath hematoma).

eliminations.label: deep circumflex iliac artery — too lateral/inferior
eliminations.detail: WHAT IT IS: a branch of the external iliac that runs along the iliac crest to supply the lower lateral abdominal wall. ❌ WHY IT FAILS: it sits far LATERAL and inferior to the rectus, not where the muscle is transected. ✅ Correct if: dissection along the iliac crest / anterior superior iliac spine.

eliminations.label: deep inguinal ring — an inguinal landmark, not in the sheath
eliminations.detail: WHAT IT IS: the opening in the transversalis fascia where the spermatic cord/round ligament leaves the abdomen, lateral to the inferior epigastric vessels. ❌ WHY IT FAILS: it's an inguinal-canal landmark, not a structure cut within the rectus during a C-section. ✅ Correct if: indirect inguinal hernia anatomy.

eliminations.label: femoral sheath — in the groin, not the abdomen
eliminations.detail: WHAT IT IS: the fascial sleeve below the inguinal ligament enclosing the femoral artery, vein, and canal. ❌ WHY IT FAILS: it lies in the thigh/groin, nowhere near a rectus incision. ✅ Correct if: femoral hernia or femoral vascular access.

eliminations.label: lateral femoral cutaneous nerve — thigh sensation
eliminations.detail: WHAT IT IS: a sensory nerve to the anterolateral thigh; its entrapment causes meralgia paresthetica. ❌ WHY IT FAILS: it passes under the inguinal ligament, far from the rectus muscle belly. ✅ Correct if: anterolateral thigh numbness/burning in pregnancy or obesity.

eliminations.label: superior epigastric artery — supplies the UPPER rectus
eliminations.detail: WHAT IT IS: the continuation of the internal thoracic artery; it descends on the posterior rectus to supply its upper part and anastomoses with the inferior epigastric. ❌ WHY IT FAILS: a LATERAL transection near the arcuate line endangers the INFERIOR epigastric where it enters; the superior epigastric is up near the costal margin. ✅ Correct if: injury high on the rectus near the xiphoid/costal margin.

reasoning: A transverse (lateral) transection of the rectus threatens the vessel entering the muscle's lateral edge
The inferior epigastric artery (from the external iliac) ascends on the posterior rectus and enters its lateral aspect near the arcuate line
Below the arcuate line there is NO posterior rectus sheath, so injury here bleeds significantly (rectus sheath hematoma)
mechanism: The inferior epigastric artery arises from the external iliac artery, ascends along the posterior surface of the rectus abdominis, and enters the muscle at its lateral aspect around the arcuate line, where it anastomoses with the superior epigastric artery. Because the rectus sheath has no posterior layer below the arcuate line, a lateral/transverse transection of the rectus there can lacerate the inferior epigastric vessels and produce a significant rectus sheath hematoma. Surgeons identify and ligate the inferior epigastric arteries bilaterally when the rectus must be cut horizontally.

difficulty.label: 📚 Pure knowledge gap
difficulty.message: First Aid Anatomy > anterior abdominal wall: rectus sheath, arcuate line, superior (internal thoracic) vs inferior (external iliac) epigastric arteries; rectus sheath hematoma below the arcuate line.

difficulty.label: 🧭 Understanding the question
difficulty.message: The ask is purely spatial: which vessel sits where the rectus is cut LATERALLY/transversely. Place each option on a mental map, then pick the one entering the rectus' lateral edge (inferior epigastric).

difficulty.label: 🔤 Vocabulary
difficulty.message: Unsure on arcuate line, rectus sheath, superior vs inferior epigastric, deep circumflex iliac, deep inguinal ring, meralgia paresthetica? Tell me which.

difficulty.label: 🧲 Pulled by a distractor
difficulty.message: Superior epigastric (F) tempts because it's the OTHER epigastric vessel — but it feeds the UPPER rectus; a lateral cut near the arcuate line hits the INFERIOR epigastric.

difficulty.label: ✍️ Something else
difficulty.message: Bank: transverse rectus transection -> ligate the inferior epigastric arteries (no posterior sheath below the arcuate line -> hematoma risk).

keywords.front: Lateral/transverse rectus transection — vessel at risk
No posterior rectus sheath below the…

keywords.back: Lateral to the inferior epigastric vessels

firstAid.detail: Anatomy > abdominal wall: from external iliac, ascends posterior rectus, enters at the arcuate line; lateral transection -> rectus sheath hematoma.
firstAid.location: Anatomy > abdominal wall: from external iliac, ascends posterior rectus, enters at the arcuate line; lateral transection -> rectus sheath hematoma.

firstAid.detail: Anatomy > superior epigastric (internal thoracic), deep circumflex iliac, deep inguinal ring, femoral sheath, lateral femoral cutaneous nerve (meralgia paresthetica).
firstAid.location: Anatomy > superior epigastric (internal thoracic), deep circumflex iliac, deep inguinal ring, femoral sheath, lateral femoral cutaneous nerve (meralgia paresthetica).

firstAid.detail: Repro > gestational diabetes -> macrosomia (>4000-4500 g), breech and large fetus as C-section indications.
firstAid.location: Repro > gestational diabetes -> macrosomia (>4000-4500 g), breech and large fetus as C-section indications.

memoryHook: Cut the rectus SIDEWAYS -> you hit the INFERIOR epigastric (climbs up from the external iliac). The superior epigastric is up top, from the internal thoracic.
